chunk_summary: Medication-associated metabolic risks in children require structured monitoring plans, focusing on high-risk drugs like atypical antipsychotics and glucocorticoids. Regular follow-ups, lab assessments, and care coordination are essential to mitigate adverse effects and optimize outcomes.
learning_objectives: Understand the metabolic risks associated with pediatric medications such as atypical antipsychotics and glucocorticoids.
Learn the recommended baseline and follow-up monitoring intervals for weight, BP, glycemia, lipids, and ALT in children on high-risk medications.
Recognize the importance of care coordination and communication between metabolic clinics and prescribing specialists.
Identify strategies to address new dysglycemia, dyslipidemia, or MASLD risk during medication therapy.
key_facts: High-risk pediatric drug classes include atypical antipsychotics, glucocorticoids, valproate, and antiretrovirals.
Baseline assessments should include weight/waist, BP, fasting glucose or HbA1c, fasting lipids, and ALT to detect early MASLD risk.
Follow-up checkpoints: weight/waist and BP at 4–6 weeks and 12 weeks; glycemia and lipids at 3 months if rapid weight gain or symptoms occur.
Annual metabolic labs are recommended, with shorter intervals during puberty or for high-risk patients (e.g., obesity, family history, prediabetes).
If dysglycemia, rising TG, or ALT elevation occurs, reassess the drug regimen and intensify family-based lifestyle interventions.
reference_quote: High-risk drug classes in pediatrics include atypical antipsychotics, chronic systemic glucocorticoids, valproate, and some antiretrovirals. Baseline assessment should include weight/waist, BP, fasting glucose or HbA1c, fasting lipids, and ALT to capture early MASLD risk.
document_key_topics: Monitoring guidelines for high-risk pediatric medications
Care coordination in metabolic risk clinics
